title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee approved several contracts and resolutions, including awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding reform, and directing staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for multiple road projects and water/sewer extensions.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkkey Avenue
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, a 2029 project that may now move to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for road settlement issues, and upcoming punch list repairs on Schofield Avenue and Fuller Street projects.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29th along with Marathon County. Activities included mailbox repairs, sign repairs, spring curb sweeping, hot mix patching, and storm damage cleanup from East Nick. Culverts failed on Jones Street requiring replacement. Significant flooding issues near Weston Avenue required dewatering pumps for about a week. Committee discussed need for permanent pump solutions and possible retention basin improvements in the Zenzer/Shore Avenue area.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance items, and continued private well sampling. Sanitary side experienced significant clear water infiltration - April showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the plant, indicating roughly 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Discussion focused on sump pump discharges to sanitary sewers and upcoming I&I study with In-Pipe Technologies to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of approximately $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million, up 6.7% due to infrastructure placed in service including Wells 7 and 8, PFAS arrangements, and water main from Weston Avenue project. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in Schofield/Rib Mountain. Approved unanimously to acknowledge.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term funding solutions for state transportation. The village needs recurring annual funding for street maintenance and snow plowing beyond one-time grants. Wisconsin is relatively affordable for automobile ownership compared to neighboring states. The committee recommended the village board adopt the resolution, approved unanimously.

Michael presented the 2026 crushing project with King Gravel as low bidder at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons ($126,900 total). Analysis showed village saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau's costs, resulting in about $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Committee discussed enforcement of existing ordinance prohibiting sump pump and other clear water discharges to sanitary sewer. Current code allows fines between $100-$1,000 per day per violation. After discussion about distinguishing unknowing versus willful violations, committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day. Staff will calculate approximate treatment costs for next meeting. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a duplex builder at 2230 Radkkey Avenue needs water/sewer connection but mains don't extend to the lot. Proposal allows temporary long lateral service until Radkkey Avenue reconstruction, when lot owner would pay assessment for main extension. Committee amended the agreement to specify reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion passed unanimously.

Village of Rothschild is leading the Military Road project from Wolfman Street to Helin Street with Steam Construction. Weston's share for water main relocation (required due to storm sewer conflicts) and sanitary sewer is estimated at $94,860. Motion to approve agreement passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910 - staff recommended MSA. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 with summer intern versus Clark Dietz at $73,730 with full-time staff - staff recommended Clark Dietz for more experienced oversight despite slightly higher cost. Committee approved both recommendations unanimously.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Village Board to adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding reform
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day
Staff to calculate treatment costs related to clear water infiltration for next meeting
Approve water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline no later than 2029
Approve intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road project ($94,860 village share)
Award Alderson/Jelnik roundabout construction services to MSA
Award Bladel Avenue construction services to Clark Dietz
Schedule special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue on May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide street lighting update at future meeting
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting following reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant to the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from chair and future items

discussions.body: The commission elected Trustee Erling as Chair, Cushing as Vice Chair (seconded by Jackson after initial procedural confusion), and Renee as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously with no opposition.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) presented on behalf of the event, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commissioner Hodal asked about room tracking and suggested getting accurate hotel room data to help future decisions. The commission noted the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. A motion to approve $5,000 (same as last year) was made by Chair Erling, seconded by Jackson, and passed unanimously. The commission requested better room tracking data for future reports.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting the winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s. The candidates visited local attractions including Mullen's Cheese and expressed appreciation for the Marathon County community.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow representatives to provide accurate hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with the commission also scheduling a joint steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan update.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished business: Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive, noting it meets all B2 zoning requirements. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to address a 36-inch underground gas pipeline easement, which reduced fill costs significantly. The building has capacity for 50-60 officers (currently 40) for future growth. Discussion included potential expansion to the north, placement of an alternate storage shed, and landscaping compliance. Motion to approve was made with conditions 2-5 as listed by staff. The motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Jennifer presented a new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per salt storage facility within wellhead protection areas - one warm weather inspection and one winter operational inspection. A standardized worksheet similar to DOT standards will be used. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and should be contacted. Commission discussed the village's shallow wells (90-110 ft) and sandy soil making contamination a concern. Motion passed unanimously to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved items and building permits
discussions.body: Both the April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps/site plans and building permits were approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Jennifer announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Discussion followed about residential TIF districts and workforce housing, with mention that Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that may be shared with the commission or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He requested the commission exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing previous concerns with the Greenheck Turner building. He also asked that the commission request annual reporting on salt shed inspections within wellhead protection zones, noting some zoning complaints are years old.
actionItems: Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan approved with staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m. for comprehensive plan
Staff to send email and calendar invite for June 1st steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging stations noted as future agenda item for fall

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new and returning members, featuring presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on; the meeting focused on introducing committee structure, departmental missions, and budget overviews.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - none for this meeting
Review of standing committee ordinance 2.04, missions, duties and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Services Department presentation by VSO Erin Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting aye.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction under ordinance section 2.04 sub-5, covering departments including Health, Social Services, and Veteran Services, plus affiliated entities North Central Health Care and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He outlined reporting relationships with the Social Services Board, Veterans Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect from environmental hazards. She highlighted 1,200 licenses overseen, public health vending machines stocked with free resources including tick kits, the Nurse Family Partnership program's first graduates, and a new homeless system response position. The department has 37.6 FTEs with about half of the $5 million budget from tax levy and significant grant funding.

Administrator Leonard provided an overview of the department's divisions including child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program addressing root causes of housing instability. The department has nearly 140 FTEs with a $22 million budget, of which approximately $7 million is tax levy.

VSO Erin Galindo reported the department increased to 3.0 FTEs and had 8,149 interactions in 2025. He emphasized PACT Act benefits, noting veterans received $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025 and $44 million annually flows into the county. Galindo highlighted suicide prevention efforts, noting Marathon County had 30 suicides in 2025 with 7 being veterans, and discussed partnership with Center for Veterans Issues for housing stability.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood) as a one-stop shop for aging and disability resources. He noted significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs rising 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat. The organization has about 60 FTEs and relies on 385-400 volunteers, with approximately 11% of budget from county levy.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Minutes from April 1st meeting approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report data to full board
Next meeting confirmed as Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)
Committee members invited to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with agenda item requests for future meetings

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and engaged in extensive discussion about the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and appointed two new members to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order and roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions
Appointments to committees and farmers market discussion

discussions.body: The board held a public hearing on increasing park impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761 for single family, $563 for duplex, and $495 for multifamily units. Jim Pitel spoke in opposition, arguing the village lacks detailed cost data to justify specific numbers. Trustee Olsen asked about fuel costs for park maintenance. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing with no opposition. Resolution 2026-015 amending the fee schedule was also approved unanimously with motion by Hermling, second by Clark.

Chief Finkey explained that Ordinance 26-008 establishes a regional inspection sticker system for food trucks. Safer fire department along with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, and Riverside agreed that one inspection from any participating department is valid across all jurisdictions, saving vendors from multiple inspections. Motion by Lope Sorrell, approved unanimously.

Extensive debate occurred over the policy language. Trustees Beck and others argued the policy should match what was voted on at the previous meeting - simply requiring 12-hour notice, camera on, and no closed session participation - without requiring justification for health, safety, or welfare reasons. An amendment to strike sentences requiring justification passed with two no votes (Hermling and Olsen). The original motion as amended passed with the same two no votes.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lope Sorrell, approved unanimously. Board agreed that future applicant resumes should be included in board packets.

Discussion only item regarding the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park after losing their previous Camp Phillips/Scoffield location. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass in the park. Renee explained the market grew from 25 stalls at the original site to 53 at Kennedy Park, and vendors now can keep their vehicles with them. Mock Miller Park was discussed but deemed too remote. Renee clarified the market operates at a profit and she secured approximately $5,000 in grants for food assistance programs. No action taken.

Approved secondhand article and jewelry dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Motion amended to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit, as the location is still under construction. Approved unanimously.

Public Works Director Michael Dolan explained the village is seeking to hire an engineering technician but has had difficulty filling the position due to a scarce workforce for that skillset in central Wisconsin. Two consultant contracts totaling approximately $108,000 for project oversight are on the agenda because of this vacancy.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment criticizing the $108,000 in outside engineering consultant costs, questioning why the engineering technician position remains unfilled, and arguing appointments to committees should be discussed publicly rather than through private communications. He also criticized the lack of detailed Kennedy Park information in the meeting packet. During the park impact fee public hearing, Pitel spoke again arguing the village lacks real operational data to justify the fee amounts.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor license ordinance 26-008 approved establishing regional inspection reciprocity
E-bike/e-moto ordinance 26-012 approved amending traffic and vehicles chapter
Nuisance ordinance 26-011 approved
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby pending occupancy and background check
Resolution 2026-014 supporting long-term transportation funding solution approved
Comprehensive Plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6:00 PM
Speed limit study for Weston Avenue to be brought back to joint meeting
Intergovernmental agreement for Ringle building inspections to be developed
Future applicant resumes to be included in board packets

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee at-large positions, and unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for disaster debris following severe weather that impacted the Town of Ringle. The fee reduction, from the standard $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton, applies to certified storm-related debris through June 17th.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments presentation by Chair Gibbs
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Ballot results announcement for Executive Committee
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster relief

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing perspective diversity, developing supervisor leadership, and accommodating preferences. Gibbs noted 34% of the board is in their first or second term. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large Executive Committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained her nomination of Leur, stating she had 'seen Supervisor Leur advocate for our county's smallest children and our oldest elders.' Paper ballots were distributed and counted. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved from the Health and Human Services Committee and Human Resources and Finance and Property Committee. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution to amend the 2026 social services budget was approved. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster relief
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal following severe weather that damaged approximately 44 homes in Ringle. The solid waste department processed about 400 tons of emergency debris. The resolution reduces tipping fees from the standard $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris, with the state waiving $13.10 and Ringle waiving $2.33. Director Hagen Busher noted Brown County and Dane County only dropped the state fee, and warned that Dane County lost FEMA reimbursement in the 1990s after fully waiving fees. The board suspended rules (unanimous) to consider the resolution directly, then approved it unanimously. Motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Special dispensation was granted for two residents to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Tolesie spoke first, requesting the county wave all fees for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their portions, and that private businesses have donated up to $40,000 in hauling services. She stated Marathon County had not provided significant financial assistance. Matt Haybeck spoke second, requesting fee waivers for the approximately 44 homeowners facing demolition, noting some insurance adjusters have not yet arrived and residents need more time.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 24-26 approved for out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to work with Community Foundation on mechanism for reimbursing unreimbursed insurance costs for landowners
Solid waste management board directed to develop clear policy on handling disaster situations within six months
Staff to track disaster debris costs and report back at May board meeting on potential timeline extension

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified resolution to the county board that would waive only reconnect fees (not other zoning or septic fees) for properties impacted by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing introduction and committee establishment
Rezoning petition 5A1: Tim Veland on behalf of James Offer and David Tract - Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley
Rezoning petition 5A2: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz - Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Rezoning petition 5A1: James Offer and David Tract - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential. The Town of Green Valley Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Rezoning petition 5A2: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the rezoning request from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Lever asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezoning would make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. He confirmed no natural area would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for tornado-impacted properties in the towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Up to 149 parcels were potentially affected. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future disasters. Supervisor Sefelt expressed concern about tracking costs. Marilyn Bend asked about mound system replacement costs. Supervisor Drebeck proposed an amendment to limit fee waivers to only reconnect permits ($160 each) rather than all zoning and septic fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor K and passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director David Hagenbusher presented an overview of the department's operations, explaining the landfill's history since 1980, the tipping fee structure, the new renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, and challenges with leachate treatment due to PFAS regulations requiring on-site treatment by 2032. He noted significant capital expenses including $6 million for 11-acre expansion construction this year.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees who manage 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest, and city of Wausau parks. He shared that Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the county economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. No one signed up for public comment at the beginning of the meeting, and no members of the public spoke during the rezoning public hearings.
actionItems: Rezoning approved for James Offer and David Tract property in Town of Green Valley - forwarded to County Board
Rezoning approved for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property in Town of McMillan - forwarded to County Board
Modified resolution to waive only reconnect fees ($160) for tornado-impacted properties forwarded to County Board for consideration
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan should update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Eau Claire River, deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and trail maintenance. The meeting addressed public concerns about fundraising transparency and meeting scheduling.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes were approved with a motion and second. Motion carried with all in favor, no opposition.

Sean Osterbrink presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park project from its inception through the May 9th ribbon cutting. He explained the village plans to contribute approximately $7 million of the $14.3 million total project for infrastructure items like bathrooms, shelter, and wellhouse. The Friends of Kennedy Park nonprofit is fundraising separately with a 10-year timeline. They currently have approximately $84,000-85,000 at the community foundation toward a $117,000 goal for field lights, and plan a public check presentation at their June tournament. Committee members discussed that the project is designed as a long-range fundraiser with no fixed completion deadline.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan presented information on tree removal from the river and mat installation over gas pipelines. Photos showed wood mats placed to protect pipelines during equipment operation. The mats are now village property with extras available for other trail projects. TC Energy (formerly ANR Pipeline/ATC) conducted site inspections during the work.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the June meeting because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. One audience member (Andy, 6504 [street name inaudible]) had attended specifically to oppose allowing dogs at the public pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.'

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches due to limited access for pressure washing equipment. The best removal tool is a sandblaster through a pressure washer, but the trailer cannot reach the trail. Committee discussed anti-vandalism coatings and seal coat options. Committee member suggested volunteer work days, noting community members love the trail. No formal action taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan Wierzba (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of proposed signage for Eau Claire River kayak launches. Three signs will be in Village of Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, Yellow Banks) and two or three in City of Schofield. Signs include QR codes for river gauge and Marathon County Parks Foundation donation link. Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve sign installation passed unanimously.

Staff requested committee interest in food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds, noting one in 13 people have food allergies. Committee agreed to bring this back for future discussion. The Mock Miller master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler, and June 11th is the last open house for the bike and pedestrian plan.

publicComment: Two members of the public commented. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating dogs belong in rivers and lakes, not public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), appearing online under the name 'Vern M', criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation, questioned the Kennedy Park funding report accuracy (noting the $8,700 figure seemed too coincidental), asked where the $10,000 grant money and Glow event funds were, and accused the Friends of Kennedy Park of being 'convoluted and misleading' with their finances.
actionItems: Kayak launch sign installation approved - will go to village board
Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate graffiti removal and cleaning options for Eau Claire River Trail
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future discussion
Mock Miller master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22nd, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved minutes and acknowledged budget reports before receiving an extensive educational presentation on the Municipal Clerk's Office operations, covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and staffing. Committee member Evan Easterling announced his resignation from the committee due to moving out of the village.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's Office budget and operations
Staff remarks and farmers market discussion

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes from the previous Finance and HR meeting was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Committee member asked about the final TIF numbers and bond payoff process. Staff member Jessica explained they are anticipating October/November for final action, that the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds (due October 1st), and confirmed the village will be able to pay off the debt. The process involves contacting Ehlers, who will notify US Associate Trust and DTC for official bondholder payment.
The April budget status report, TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports, and legal details report were acknowledged together. Motion by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented extensively on clerk office operations. Key points included: staffing reduced from four full-time to two full-time plus one part-time (Leah); elections budget varies significantly between even years (presidential/gubernatorial) and odd years; Badger Books cost approximately $11,000 for four units; 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is handled at the clerk's office counter; pet licensing was brought in-house in 2025 after 12 years with third-party Pet Data, saving approximately $5,495 annually; and the quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing to reach 5,089 postal patrons.

Committee discussed the fee increase for neutered dogs from $10 to $20. Pam explained Marathon County requested the increase because they pay about $100,000 annually to the Humane Society but were only collecting $65,000 from municipalities. Discussion also covered the non-dog stray contract costing $175 per animal, including eight mice that were picked up. A committee member suggested requiring law enforcement to bring in strays rather than allowing residents to do so directly.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well, with her present for 6-7 hours on Saturdays since opening. Tuesday markets have had some weather challenges. New additions include coffee vendors, food trucks, and craft vendors. Produce vendors expected mid to end of June. Full vendor capacity reported. Committee member noted vendors were 'very happy' with the setup and location. Discussion of parking concerns on Alderson (west side only now allows parking) and Newport streets.

Evan Easterling announced he is stepping down from the committee as his last meeting, as he and his wife are moving away from Weston but staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: CDA resolution needed to call TIF bonds, anticipated summer 2025
Poll worker chief inspector pay increase to $15/hour to be proposed for next two-year election term
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee spots
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to the nuisance ordinance and e-bike regulations, discussed overnight parking regulations without taking action, and authorized razing a condemned structure. The committee also approved fire inspection requirements for mobile food vendors and addressed several property addressing corrections.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Written communications and department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: Code Enforcement Officer Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt noted the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer. Two SROs work with zoning violations during summer. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments to the nuisance ordinance, including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet) and compliance with jurisdictional authority. Olsen made a motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Committee discussed the model e-bike ordinance for Chapter 82. Chief Hunt recommended prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles) from roadways and sidewalks due to safety concerns, noting they are essentially unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion included whether electric scooters used by children should be treated differently. Olsen motioned to recommend approval with the addition that emotos are prohibited from operating on roadways, sidewalks, trails, or any public property. Seconded by Chwanka. Motion passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour no parking window (4 AM to 4 PM, October 31-May 1) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities which typically have 4-5 hour windows overnight. DPW Director Michael suggested possibly eliminating the broad restriction and instead signing only the 6-8 problem areas. Chief Hunt advocated keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. Committee directed Michael to develop specific exception areas for the July meeting; no action taken.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest High School parking lot. Staff estimated cost at approximately $2,000-3,000 for a basic crosswalk or $25,000-30,000 for a lighted system. Committee members expressed reluctance to set precedent of paying for crosswalks on request. Olsen motioned that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the installation cost, the committee recommends board approval. Seconded by Chwanka. Motion passed unanimously. DPW Director will contact Mount Olive regarding cost sharing.

Fire Chief explained a new regional fire inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and SAFER. One inspection and sticker is valid across all participating municipalities. Chief noted a recent inspection found significant safety violations including improper LP gas piping. Wausau opted not to participate. Chwanka motioned to approve Ordinance 26-8; seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicowski Street (duplex needing split addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue near a new duplex. Chief Hunt noted an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicowski with eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze order on a structure abandoned since 2010-2011. The property was previously considered for fire department training burn but was deemed too deteriorated for safe exercise. Current owners have been housing chickens in the condemned structure. Owners prefer the village proceed with demolition. Costs will be placed on the tax roll. Olsen motioned, Campkey seconded. Motion passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding multiple agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, predicting the grassy areas will be destroyed by vehicles parking on grass, which he believes violates village code. He questioned whether ADA-accessible parking spaces on grass comply with requirements for firm, level surfaces. He asked where the food truck fire inspection requirement originated. He also questioned the clarity of mobile food vendor regulations regarding farmers market days and expressed concern that some code enforcement cases have been pending for years without visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to board of trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition to board of trustees
DPW Director to identify specific problem areas for overnight parking restrictions for July meeting
DPW Director to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost sharing
Recommend Ordinance 26-8 (mobile food vendor fire inspections) to board
Recommend address corrections to board of trustees
Authorize staff to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber regarding outdoor furniture and dumpster

title: Public Works Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, this meeting was scheduled to address a public hearing on special assessments for the Alderson St and Jelinek Ave intersection project, along with multiple infrastructure resolutions and project updates. The meeting was significant as it involved property owner assessments for driveway approach construction related to a new roundabout installation.

discussions.item: Alderson St and Jelinek Ave Intersection Project Special Assessments Public Hearing
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to conduct a public hearing on special assessments for driveway approach construction related to a project replacing the existing 4-way stop intersection with a compact roundabout. Property owners were expected to have opportunity for testimony and questions, with assessments calculated at $8.61 per square foot for concrete driveway aprons and prorated for driveways installed within the past 20 years.

The committee was set to consider approval of the 2025 Compliance Maintenance Annual Report for Weston Sewer Utility for submission to the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources. This is a required annual regulatory compliance report.

The committee was expected to consider an authorizing resolution for the reconstruction of Bloedel Ave from Pine Park St to Alderson St. This follows a special assessment hearing held on May 28, 2026.

discussions.item: Resolution 2026-018 – Alderson St and Jelinek Ave Intersection Project
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to consider authorization for the Alderson St and Jelinek Ave intersection project, which involves replacing the 4-way stop with a roundabout, along with water main relocation, sanitary sewer improvements, and storm sewer replacement.
The committee was set to review quotes for topsoil screening services. The agenda did not provide details on the scope or vendors submitting quotes.
The committee was scheduled to review paving quotes for Shirley Ave. Specific details on project scope were not included in the agenda.

discussions.item: Contract Amendment No. 1 with MSA for Right of Way Services
discussions.body: The committee was expected to consider a contract amendment with MSA for right of way services related to the Alderson St and Jelinek Ave intersection project.

discussions.item: Change Order No. 1 for Alderson St and Jelinek Ave Project
discussions.body: The committee was set to review the first change order for the Alderson St and Jelinek Ave intersection project. Details on the nature of the change order were not specified in the agenda.

overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to hold its regular meeting on June 8, 2026, with significant financial matters including the preliminary 2026-27 budget and a debt defeasance resolution for 2022 bonds. The board was also expected to consider multiple policy updates, employee handbook changes, meal price adjustments, and a facilities project at Riverview, along with receiving updates on summer learning programs and changes to the DPI Community Eligibility Provision program.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in approximately 2 minutes. The presentation was expected to include a marked-up version showing suggested edits and a clean version with changes accepted for board review and approval.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
actionItems: Bike Rack Request Form approved for implementation by Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee
Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects

Mayoral appointments confirmed to Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board
